
  The Long Way Home

  The morning comes in shades of gray,
  A quiet hush before the day,
  The kettle hums its patient song,
  And shadows where they don't belong.

  I walked the road I used to know,
  When I was younger, long ago,
  The fences leaning, paint worn thin,
  The same old dog, the same old grin.

  The river kept its steady pace,
  Reflecting back a stranger's face,
  And every stone I used to skip
  Lay still beneath the water's lip.

  We measure years in things that stay,
  In what we keep and give away,
  In doors we open, doors we close,
  In gardens where the wild thing grows.

  So let the evening fold its wings,
  And let me count the simple things:
  A loaf of bread, a friend, a light,
  A door left open through the night.

  And when at last the road runs out,
  And all my wandering and doubt
  Have settled like the evening sun,
  I'll know that home was where I'd come.

✻ Churned for 11s

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
❯
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  ⏵⏵ auto mode on (shift+tab to cycle) · ← for agents
